_meta.authoringNotes: KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Diseases Guideline used throughout. Pediatric epidemiology per Eddy & Symons Lancet 2003 (PMID 12241376). Membranous PLA2R discovery per Beck et al. NEJM 2009 (PMID 19564355). Rituximab per MENTOR trial Fervenza et al. NEJM 2019 (PMID 31597036). FSGS classification per D'Agati et al. NEJM 2011 (PMID 22087681). Adult primary causes distribution per O'Shaughnessy et al. NDT 2017. Thromboembolism risk per Lionaki et al. CJASN 2012 (PMID 22383749).

h1Title: Nephrotic Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Nephrotic syndrome is a clinical state defined by heavy urinary protein loss exceeding 3.5 g per 24 hours in adults (or urine protein/creatinine ratio above 2.0 mg/mg in children), serum albumin below 3.0 g/dL, peripheral edema, and hyperlipidemia. The underlying defect is increased glomerular permeability driven by podocyte injury, immune-complex deposition, or genetic protein mutation. Incidence is roughly 2-7 per 100,000 children per year and 3 per 100,000 adults per year. The pediatric form responds dramatically to corticosteroids in 80-90% of cases; in adults, primary causes such as membranous nephropathy and focal segmental glomerulosclerosis carry a 30-50% risk of progressive kidney disease over a decade. Modern treatment combines immunosuppression targeted to the underlying lesion, renin-angiotensin blockade to reduce proteinuria, and aggressive management of thromboembolic and cardiovascular risk.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence 2-7 per 100,000 children and approximately 3 per 100,000 adults in the United States and Europe
keyStats.demographics: Children: 60-70% are male; peak age 2-6 years. Adults: membranous nephropathy peaks at 50-60 years, FSGS at 30-50 years, with higher rates in Black populations (FSGS 3-5× higher than White)
keyStats.avgAge: Children: peak onset 2-6 years; adults: bimodal at 30-50 and 60-70 years
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 16 cases per 100,000 children worldwide carry chronic or relapsing disease; global adult prevalence not precisely measured but estimated at hundreds of thousands

definition: Nephrotic syndrome (ICD-10: N04) is a glomerular disorder characterized by the triad of heavy proteinuria (>3.5 g/24 h in adults, urine protein/creatinine ratio >2.0 mg/mg in children), hypoalbuminemia (<3.0 g/dL), and peripheral edema, frequently accompanied by hyperlipidemia and a hypercoagulable state. The unifying pathophysiology is loss of the glomerular filtration barrier — specifically podocyte foot-process effacement and slit-diaphragm disruption — which allows albumin and other plasma proteins to spill into urine. In children, more than 85% of cases are minimal change disease, a podocytopathy responsive to corticosteroids. In adults, the spectrum is broader: primary glomerular diseases (membranous nephropathy, focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, minimal change disease, IgA-related variants) account for half of cases, while secondary causes (diabetes mellitus, systemic lupus erythematosus, amyloidosis, hepatitis B and C, HIV, malignancy, and certain drugs) explain the rest. The condition is managed by nephrology, with kidney biopsy as the definitive diagnostic step in adults and atypical pediatric presentations. Distinction from nephritic syndrome (hematuria-dominant glomerular inflammation) is fundamental: nephrotic disease is proteinuria-dominant with bland urinary sediment, while nephritic disease features active sediment with red-cell casts.

typesClassification.description: Diffuse podocyte foot-process effacement on electron microscopy with normal light microscopy. Accounts for >85% of pediatric and 10-15% of adult primary nephrotic syndrome. Responds to oral prednisolone in 80-90% within 4-8 weeks; relapsing forms managed with calcineurin inhibitors, mycophenolate, or rituximab.

Segmental scarring in some glomeruli on light microscopy. Primary FSGS is a podocytopathy with circulating permeability factor; secondary FSGS results from hyperfiltration (obesity, reflux, single kidney) or viral, drug, or genetic causes. Black patients have 3-5× higher incidence linked to APOL1 risk variants. 30-50% progress to end-stage kidney disease without effective treatment.

Subepithelial immune-complex deposits along the glomerular basement membrane. Most adult primary cases are PLA2R-antibody driven (70-80%). One-third remit spontaneously, one-third have persistent proteinuria, and one-third progress. Modern treatment is rituximab (MENTOR trial) or cyclophosphamide-steroid regimens.

Most common cause of nephrotic-range proteinuria in adults globally. Years of poorly controlled type 1 or type 2 diabetes produce Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules, expanded mesangium, and progressive proteinuria. Managed with RAAS blockade, SGLT2 inhibitors, and glycemic control rather than immunosuppression.

Subepithelial immune-complex deposits resembling primary MN, occurring in 10-20% of lupus nephritis cases. Often presents with full nephrotic syndrome and is treated with mycophenolate or rituximab combined with steroids.

Extracellular deposition of misfolded protein in glomeruli. AL amyloidosis from plasma cell dyscrasia and AA amyloidosis from chronic inflammation present with nephrotic syndrome and require treatment of the underlying clonal or inflammatory disease.

primarySymptoms: Bilateral lower-extremity pitting edema starting at the ankles, progressing to the calves, thighs, and abdominal wall, often worst at end of day and improved overnight.
Periorbital puffiness on waking, frequently the earliest sign in children and mistaken for allergic conjunctivitis.
Foamy or frothy urine that persists in the toilet bowl, reflecting heavy proteinuria.
Rapid weight gain of 2-10 kg over days to weeks from fluid retention, with tightening of clothing and shoes.
Generalized swelling (anasarca) with abdominal distension, scrotal or labial edema, and pleural effusions producing dyspnea in severe cases.
Fatigue, malaise, reduced appetite, and easy bruising linked to protein loss, hypoalbuminemia, and altered coagulation.
Decreased urine output and dark concentrated urine during marked fluid retention; conversely, polyuria from diuretic therapy once started.
Abdominal pain in children from intestinal-wall edema or, rarely, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (pneumococcal).
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent foamy urine that does not clear with flushing — easiest at-home indicator of significant proteinuria
Unexplained ankle swelling lasting beyond a few days in a previously well child or adult
Periorbital puffiness present on waking in a child with no allergic history
Unintentional weight gain of more than 2 kg over a week without dietary change
Detection of 2+ to 4+ protein on a routine dipstick urinalysis
emergencySigns: Acute shortness of breath with bilateral pleural effusions or pulmonary edema requiring urgent diuresis and oxygen
Acute unilateral leg swelling with calf tenderness — deep vein thrombosis is markedly increased in nephrotic syndrome (renal-vein thrombosis particularly in membranous nephropathy)
Sudden severe abdominal pain with fever in a nephrotic child — exclude spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
Acute kidney injury with oliguria, rising creatinine, and hyperkalemia — may reflect renal-vein thrombosis, severe intravascular depletion, or crescentic transformation
Severe hypoalbuminemia (<1.5 g/dL) with signs of circulatory collapse — requires urgent inpatient management

causes.description: Idiopathic diffuse podocyte foot-process effacement, possibly driven by a circulating permeability factor and T-cell dysregulation. Causes more than 85% of pediatric and 10-15% of adult primary nephrotic syndrome. May follow viral illness or atopy.

causes.cause: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (primary, secondary, and genetic)
causes.description: Primary FSGS is a podocytopathy with circulating permeability factor; secondary FSGS results from adaptive hyperfiltration in obesity, reflux nephropathy, solitary kidney, or after viral and drug exposure (HIV, pamidronate, anabolic steroids). Genetic FSGS arises from podocyte gene mutations (NPHS1, NPHS2, INF2, ACTN4).

Anti-PLA2R or anti-THSD7A antibodies bind antigens on podocyte foot processes, forming subepithelial immune-complex deposits that damage the glomerular basement membrane. Secondary forms accompany hepatitis B, lupus, malignancy (especially in patients over 65), and certain drugs.

The leading cause of nephrotic-range proteinuria globally. Chronic hyperglycemia drives mesangial expansion, glomerular basement membrane thickening, podocyte loss, and nodular sclerosis. Onset typically 10-20 years into type 1 diabetes; earlier in type 2 with poor control or hypertension.

causes.cause: Systemic lupus erythematosus (class V membranous lupus)
causes.description: Immune-complex deposition in the subepithelial space in 10-20% of patients with lupus nephritis. Anti-dsDNA antibodies, low complement, and other features of SLE help distinguish from primary membranous nephropathy.

Deposition of misfolded immunoglobulin light chain (AL) or serum amyloid A (AA) protein in glomeruli. AL amyloidosis arises from a plasma cell dyscrasia; AA from chronic inflammation (rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, familial Mediterranean fever, chronic infection).

HIV-associated nephropathy (collapsing FSGS), hepatitis B (membranous nephropathy, membranoproliferative pattern), hepatitis C (membranoproliferative with cryoglobulinemia), NSAIDs (minimal change/membranous), gold, penicillamine, lithium, and bisphosphonates all produce nephrotic syndromes.

riskFactors.description: Peak pediatric incidence is between 2 and 6 years, when minimal change disease is the dominant histology. Boys are affected roughly twice as often as girls in this age band.

Two APOL1 risk alleles increase the risk of FSGS, HIV-associated nephropathy, and hypertensive kidney disease by 10-17×, contributing to the 3-5× higher incidence of FSGS in Black populations in the US and West Africa.
Sustained HbA1c above 8% and uncontrolled blood pressure accelerate diabetic kidney disease. SGLT2 inhibitors and RAAS blockade significantly reduce progression risk.
Approximately 50% of SLE patients develop kidney involvement, and 10-20% present with class V (membranous) lupus nephritis manifesting as nephrotic syndrome.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic viral infection (HIV, hepatitis B, hepatitis C)
riskFactors.description: Untreated HIV causes collapsing FSGS in patients with APOL1 risk alleles. Hepatitis B causes membranous nephropathy. Hepatitis C produces membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis with mixed cryoglobulinemia. Antiviral treatment can reverse these.
Class III obesity (BMI ≥40) increases secondary FSGS risk through hyperfiltration injury. Weight loss reduces proteinuria substantially in obesity-related glomerulopathy.
Up to 20% of older adults with membranous nephropathy have an associated solid tumor (lung, breast, colon, prostate). Age-appropriate cancer screening is mandatory at diagnosis.

riskFactors.factor: Prior NSAID, gold, penicillamine, lithium, or bisphosphonate exposure
riskFactors.description: Several drugs produce nephrotic syndrome through podocyte injury (NSAIDs, lithium) or immune-complex membranous patterns (gold, penicillamine). Drug withdrawal typically resolves the proteinuria over weeks to months.

affectedDemographics: Children: incidence 2-7 per 100,000 per year, peaking at age 2-6 years; boys affected 2:1 versus girls in the youngest age band.
Adult primary nephrotic syndrome: incidence approximately 3 per 100,000 per year; mean age at presentation 50-55 years for membranous nephropathy and 35-50 years for FSGS.
Ethnic distribution: Black populations have 3-5× higher FSGS incidence linked to APOL1 G1/G2 risk alleles; Asian populations have higher rates of IgA-related and minimal change disease.
Comorbidity: diabetic kidney disease is the single most common cause of nephrotic-range proteinuria globally; affects approximately 40% of patients with longstanding type 1 or type 2 diabetes.
diagnosisOverview: Initial evaluation confirms nephrotic-range proteinuria with a 24-hour urine collection or spot urine protein/creatinine ratio (>3.5 g/24 h or PCR >3.5 mg/mg in adults; >2.0 mg/mg in children), measures serum albumin (<3.0 g/dL confirms hypoalbuminemia), and assesses kidney function (creatinine, eGFR), lipid panel, urinalysis with microscopy (bland sediment supports primary nephrotic disease; red-cell casts shift toward a mixed nephritic-nephrotic picture), and serum complement (C3, C4). Secondary-cause screening is mandatory: HbA1c, hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C antibody, HIV serology, antinuclear antibody, anti-dsDNA, complement, anti-PLA2R antibody (positive in 70-80% of primary membranous nephropathy), serum free light chains and immunofixation (amyloidosis), and age-appropriate cancer screening in adults over 50. In children with classic presentation (age 2-10, normal kidney function, no hematuria, complement normal), empiric corticosteroid therapy is started without biopsy because minimal change disease is overwhelmingly likely. In adults, atypical pediatric cases, and steroid-resistant disease, percutaneous kidney biopsy provides definitive light microscopy, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy. Imaging (renal ultrasound, Doppler for renal-vein thrombosis) screens for anatomic causes and complications. KDIGO 2021 guideline frames diagnostic workflow.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine protein/creatinine ratio (UPCR) on spot urine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies proteinuria; UPCR >3.5 mg/mg confirms nephrotic range in adults
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single mid-morning urine sample provided at the lab; result within hours. Replaces less-practical 24-hour collection in most settings.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum albumin, creatinine, electrolytes, lipid panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms hypoalbuminemia, assesses kidney function, identifies hyperlipidemia, and evaluates electrolyte derangements (hyponatremia, hypocalcemia from albumin loss)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single fasting blood draw; result same day in most labs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies hematuria, dysmorphic red cells, red-cell casts (nephritic features), and lipiduria (oval fat bodies, Maltese crosses)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh urine sample examined under microscope; result within 1-2 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses primary membranous nephropathy without biopsy in many cases; titres track disease activity and treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single serum blood draw; results within 1-2 weeks. Sensitivity 70-80% in primary membranous nephropathy.

diagnosticTests.test: Hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C antibody, HIV serology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for viral causes of secondary nephrotic syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1-7 days. Mandatory before starting immunosuppression.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies lupus nephritis; low C3 and C4 with positive anti-dsDNA supports the diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results within 1-2 weeks. Repeated over time to monitor disease activity.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum and urine free light chains, immunofixation
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes AL amyloidosis and other monoclonal gammopathies in adults over 40
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw and urine sample; results within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for histologic diagnosis in adults and steroid-resistant or atypical pediatric cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound-guided needle biopsy under local anesthetic, performed as a day-case. Bed rest for 6-24 hours afterward; gross hematuria in 5-10%, major bleeding under 1%. Tissue analyzed by light microscopy, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy; report within 5-10 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment has three pillars: targeted immunosuppression for primary glomerular diseases, treatment of the underlying cause for secondary disease, and supportive therapy to reduce proteinuria, fluid overload, lipid abnormalities, and thromboembolic risk. In children with classic minimal change disease, oral prednisolone (60 mg/m² per day, max 60 mg, for 4-6 weeks, then alternate-day taper) produces complete remission in 80-90% within 8 weeks. Frequent relapsers and steroid-dependent children receive calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine), mycophenolate mofetil, or rituximab. In adults with primary FSGS, prolonged high-dose corticosteroids (1 mg/kg/day for 12-16 weeks) followed by taper produce remission in roughly 50%; calcineurin inhibitors are added for steroid-resistant cases. In primary membranous nephropathy, the MENTOR trial (NEJM 2019) established rituximab as non-inferior to cyclosporine and superior in maintaining remission, supporting rituximab as first-line in moderate-to-high-risk disease per KDIGO 2021. Cyclophosphamide-steroid (modified Ponticelli regimen) remains an option in high-risk patients. Secondary disease responds to treatment of the underlying cause: tight glycemic control plus RAAS blockade and SGLT2 inhibitors in diabetic kidney disease; mycophenolate or rituximab plus steroids in class V lupus nephritis; antiviral therapy in hepatitis B-related membranous nephropathy. Supportive therapy applies to all: ACE inhibitor or ARB to maximally tolerated dose for antiproteinuric effect, salt restriction (<2 g sodium/day), loop diuretics for edema, statins for hyperlipidemia, prophylactic anticoagulation when serum albumin falls below 2.0-2.5 g/dL or in high-risk membranous nephropathy, vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza, varicella, hepatitis B), and infection vigilance.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone (60 mg/m²/day, max 60 mg, 4-6 weeks, then alternate-day taper) — pediatric minimal change disease
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy in children with classic presentation. Induces complete remission in 80-90% within 8 weeks. Tapered slowly over 4-6 months to reduce relapse risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete remission 80-90% in 8 weeks; relapse rate 60-70% over the first year; 30% become frequent relapsers

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day, max 80 mg, 12-16 weeks then taper) — adult primary FSGS or minimal change disease
medicalTreatments.description: Prolonged induction course given for adult primary FSGS or adult-onset minimal change disease. Tapered over 6 months total.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission 40-60% in adult FSGS; relapses 30-50% require maintenance therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (1 g IV ×2, separated by 14 days, repeated at 6 months)
medicalTreatments.description: B-cell depletion. First-line in primary membranous nephropathy with persistent proteinuria or rising anti-PLA2R titres (MENTOR trial, NEJM 2019); also used in steroid-dependent minimal change disease and refractory FSGS.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission 60-65% at 24 months in primary membranous nephropathy; durable response in frequent-relapsing pediatric MCD

medicalTreatments.name: Tacrolimus (0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day, target trough 5-8 ng/mL) or cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day, target trough 100-175 ng/mL)
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitors used in steroid-dependent or steroid-resistant disease. Effective in minimal change disease, FSGS, and membranous nephropathy. Monitor for nephrotoxicity and metabolic effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission 50-70%; relapses 40-60% after withdrawal

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil (1-1.5 g twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative immunosuppressant for steroid-dependent minimal change disease, lupus nephritis class V, and FSGS. Lower nephrotoxicity than calcineurin inhibitors.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission 50-65% in selected populations; first-line maintenance in lupus nephritis

medicalTreatments.name: Modified Ponticelli regimen (alternating monthly cyclophosphamide and methylprednisolone for 6 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Established high-efficacy regimen for high-risk primary membranous nephropathy. Effective but with significant toxicity (infection, infertility, bladder cancer); reserved for selected patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission 60-80% at 24 months; higher remission than rituximab in some high-risk groups

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitor or ARB at maximally tolerated dose
medicalTreatments.description: Renin-angiotensin blockade reduces intraglomerular pressure and proteinuria by 30-40%, regardless of histology. Often combined with SGLT2 inhibitor in diabetic kidney disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Proteinuria reduction 30-40%; slows GFR decline in long-term studies

medicalTreatments.name: SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin 10 mg or empagliflozin 10 mg once daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Now standard adjunct for chronic kidney disease with proteinuria. DAPA-CKD and EMPA-KIDNEY trials demonstrated reduced progression to dialysis and cardiovascular events independent of diabetes status.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces composite kidney failure or death by approximately 30% in proteinuric CKD

medicalTreatments.name: Loop diuretic (furosemide 40-160 mg/day or torsemide 10-40 mg/day; IV if oral malabsorption)
medicalTreatments.description: Mobilizes edema. Often combined with thiazide for synergistic effect (metolazone, hydrochlorothiazide) and albumin infusion in resistant cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in most patients; targets 0.5-1 kg per day weight loss to avoid intravascular depletion

medicalTreatments.name: Atorvastatin or rosuvastatin
medicalTreatments.description: Manages secondary hyperlipidemia from nephrotic syndrome. Reduces LDL and cardiovascular risk during prolonged proteinuric phases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: LDL reduction 30-50%; cardiovascular event reduction in long-term proteinuric disease

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic anticoagulation (warfarin or DOAC) when serum albumin <2.0-2.5 g/dL
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces thromboembolic risk in nephrotic syndrome, especially primary membranous nephropathy where renal-vein thrombosis incidence approaches 30%. Decision-making uses risk scores incorporating proteinuria, albumin, and bleeding risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 60-70% relative risk reduction for venous thromboembolism in high-risk cohorts

surgicalOptions.name: Kidney transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment for patients who progress to end-stage kidney disease from FSGS, membranous nephropathy, lupus nephritis, or diabetic kidney disease. Recurrence of primary FSGS in the transplant occurs in 20-40% and requires plasmapheresis or rituximab.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year graft survival 80-90% for living-donor and 70-80% for deceased-donor kidneys

surgicalOptions.name: Hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis vascular/peritoneal access placement
surgicalOptions.description: Required when GFR falls below 10-15 mL/min/1.73 m². Arteriovenous fistula or graft for hemodialysis; peritoneal catheter for PD.
surgicalOptions.successRate: AVF primary patency 60-70% at 1 year; PD catheter survival 75-85% at 2 years

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary salt restriction and weight monitoring
alternativeTreatments.description: Sodium below 2 g per day reduces edema and supports diuretic action. Daily home weights flag fluid retention early.

alternativeTreatments.name: Plasmapheresis for recurrent FSGS post-transplant
alternativeTreatments.description: Removal of circulating permeability factor reduces proteinuria in recurrent FSGS after kidney transplantation; often combined with rituximab.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with nephrotic syndrome require nephrology evaluation. Specialist input is essential for biopsy decisions in adults, choice of immunosuppression, monitoring for treatment toxicity and thromboembolic complications, and recognition of secondary causes that require systemic therapy. Pediatric nephrology manages childhood-onset disease through induction, taper, and relapse.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a nephrologist or pediatric nephrologist affiliated with a center that performs percutaneous kidney biopsy, has on-site renal pathology with immunofluorescence and electron microscopy, and a glomerular disease clinic familiar with rituximab, cyclophosphamide, and calcineurin inhibitor regimens. Centers participating in registries (NephCure, Cure GN) typically follow current KDIGO guidance.
hospitalCriteria: Renal pathology with immunofluorescence and electron microscopy
Pharmacy access to rituximab, calcineurin inhibitors, cyclophosphamide, and IV albumin
keyFacilities: Academic nephrology centers with glomerular disease programs
Specialist outpatient infusion units for rituximab
preventionStrategies: Maintain optimal glycemic control (HbA1c <7%) in diabetes to prevent diabetic kidney disease, the leading cause of nephrotic-range proteinuria globally.
Treat hepatitis B and hepatitis C with effective antivirals to prevent virus-associated glomerular disease.
Initiate antiretroviral therapy promptly in HIV infection to prevent HIV-associated nephropathy.
Achieve blood pressure control below 130/80 mmHg with RAAS-blocking agents where feasible.
Limit chronic NSAID use, particularly in patients with predisposing factors (older age, hypertension, prior kidney disease).
Pneumococcal and influenza vaccination in patients with established proteinuria to reduce infection-triggered complications.
lifestyleModifications: Restrict dietary sodium to under 2 g (87 mmol) per day to reduce edema and improve diuretic response.
Weigh daily at the same time and record; report >2 kg gain in a week to the nephrology team.
Adhere strictly to immunosuppression schedules and rituximab infusion appointments.
Avoid NSAIDs unless explicitly approved by the nephrology team.
Complete pneumococcal (PCV13 then PPSV23), influenza, hepatitis B, and varicella vaccinations before starting major immunosuppression.
Stop smoking and limit alcohol to reduce cardiovascular and infection risk during prolonged proteinuria.
Use compression stockings during prolonged immobility (long flights, post-operative) given thrombosis risk.

recommended: Plant-forward diet rich in vegetables, fruits, legumes, and whole grains
Adequate protein intake of 0.8-1.0 g/kg/day in non-CKD-progressing nephrotic syndrome; higher (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) during relapse only under dietitian supervision
Lean fish, poultry, and plant proteins over red and processed meat
Omega-3-rich foods (salmon, sardines, flaxseed, walnuts) for cardiovascular protection
avoid: Salt-heavy processed foods, canned soups, restaurant meals, and pickles
Excess animal protein loading that can worsen hyperfiltration in residual CKD
Sugar-sweetened beverages that complicate metabolic management
Grapefruit and Seville orange when on tacrolimus or cyclosporine (alters drug levels)

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic activity (walking, cycling, swimming) of 150 minutes per week is encouraged once major edema is controlled. Avoid contact sports during active heavy proteinuria due to bleeding risk if anticoagulated. Resume normal activity once stable; resistance training is safe and helps offset corticosteroid-induced muscle loss.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed immunosuppression at the same time each day; tacrolimus and cyclosporine on an empty stomach with consistent timing for stable levels.
Check daily weight and report sudden gains.
Use a urine dipstick at home (where supplied) to track proteinuria trend during remission attempts.
Keep a symptom diary including edema severity, urine output, and side effects of medication.
Stay current on all prescribed vaccinations and infection-prevention measures.
Maintain hydration with 1.5-2 L water daily unless instructed otherwise during severe fluid retention.
prognosis: Outcome depends on histology, age, response to initial therapy, and degree of kidney function at presentation. Pediatric minimal change disease has the best prognosis: 80-90% achieve complete remission with corticosteroids, and even frequent relapsers usually maintain long-term kidney function with appropriate maintenance therapy. Adult minimal change disease responds similarly but with more relapses. Primary FSGS carries a 30-50% risk of progression to end-stage kidney disease over 10 years; partial or complete remission with treatment improves 10-year kidney survival to 80-90% versus 30-50% in unresponsive patients. Primary membranous nephropathy follows a thirds rule: roughly one-third remit spontaneously, one-third have persistent proteinuria with stable kidney function, and one-third progress; modern rituximab-based treatment improves long-term outcomes substantially. Diabetic kidney disease, lupus class V, and amyloidosis follow trajectories of their underlying systemic disease. Across all forms, achieving complete remission (proteinuria <0.3 g/day) is the strongest predictor of long-term kidney preservation, and SGLT2 inhibitors plus RAAS blockade now substantially slow progression even in residual proteinuria.
recoveryTimeline: Children with minimal change disease typically remit within 2-4 weeks of starting prednisolone, with edema resolution and proteinuria clearance. Adult FSGS responses take 8-16 weeks of high-dose steroid therapy. Membranous nephropathy responds more slowly: rituximab effects on anti-PLA2R titres become apparent at 3-6 months and proteinuria clearance often takes 12-24 months. Maintenance immunosuppression and follow-up extend for years even after remission.
complications: Venous thromboembolism, especially renal-vein thrombosis in membranous nephropathy (incidence 10-30%); prophylactic anticoagulation in high-risk patients reduces this.

Infections (peritonitis, pneumonia, cellulitis), driven by hypogammaglobulinemia, immunosuppression, and edema-related skin breakdown; pneumococcal and influenza vaccination plus prompt treatment of febrile episodes.
Acute kidney injury from intravascular depletion, NSAIDs, calcineurin inhibitor toxicity, or thrombotic occlusion.
Hyperlipidemia and accelerated cardiovascular disease from chronic proteinuria.
Corticosteroid toxicity (cushingoid features, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, growth failure in children) — minimized by alternate-day dosing and steroid-sparing agents.
Progression to chronic kidney disease and end-stage kidney disease, particularly in FSGS, membranous nephropathy, and untreated secondary causes.

supportResources.name: NephCure Kidney International
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organization focused on nephrotic syndrome, FSGS, and minimal change disease with educational resources and clinical trial directory.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation — Nephrotic Syndrome
supportResources.description: Patient education on causes, treatment, and living with nephrotic syndrome.

supportResources.name: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guideline 2021
supportResources.description: Authoritative international clinical practice guideline for glomerular diseases including all major nephrotic syndromes.

supportResources.name: Cure GN (CureGN consortium)
supportResources.description: International registry and longitudinal cohort study for adults and children with primary glomerular disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nephritic Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nephritic syndrome features hematuria with red-cell casts, hypertension, and reduced glomerular filtration from glomerular inflammation, with proteinuria typically under 3.5 g/day. Nephrotic syndrome is dominated by heavy proteinuria (>3.5 g/day), hypoalbuminemia, edema, and a bland urinary sediment without red-cell casts.

confusedWithConditions.name: Minimal Change Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Minimal change disease is the specific histologic subtype causing 85% of childhood and 10-15% of adult nephrotic syndrome. The clinical syndrome of nephrotic syndrome is the broader presentation; minimal change disease is one of several causes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Membranous nephropathy is one specific cause of nephrotic syndrome with subepithelial immune-complex deposits, often anti-PLA2R positive. Nephrotic syndrome is the clinical phenotype that can result from membranous nephropathy or several other glomerular diseases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diabetic Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diabetic nephropathy is the leading cause of nephrotic-range proteinuria globally and typically does not require biopsy when classical features (long-standing diabetes, retinopathy, gradual proteinuria onset) are present. Primary nephrotic syndromes lack these systemic markers.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus nephritis presents with proteinuria, often with hematuria, hypertension, and active urinary sediment, in the setting of systemic lupus features (rash, arthritis, low complement, positive ANA/dsDNA). Class V (membranous) lupus nephritis can present as pure nephrotic syndrome and is distinguished by lupus serology.

relatedConditions.name: Nephritic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: The opposite glomerular syndrome — distinguishing nephritic from nephrotic features is the first step in glomerular disease evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Minimal Change Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common cause of childhood nephrotic syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Common cause of adult nephrotic syndrome treated with rituximab in modern practice.

relatedConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Can present with mixed nephritic-nephrotic features; managed in the same nephrology clinics.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Leading cause of nephrotic-range proteinuria globally; managed without immunosuppression.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Class V (membranous) lupus nephritis presents as nephrotic syndrome and requires distinction from primary membranous disease.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Kidney Injury
relatedConditions.relevance: AKI complicates nephrotic syndrome through hypovolemia, drug toxicity, and renal-vein thrombosis.

faqs.question: What is nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Nephrotic syndrome is a kidney disorder defined by heavy protein loss in urine (over 3.5 g/24 hours in adults), low serum albumin, peripheral edema, and high blood cholesterol. The underlying problem is a leaky glomerular filter, often from podocyte injury or immune-complex deposits.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: The classic symptoms are foamy urine, swelling of the ankles, legs, and face (especially around the eyes on waking), rapid weight gain from fluid retention, fatigue, and reduced appetite. Severe cases develop generalized swelling (anasarca), shortness of breath, and abdominal distension.

faqs.question: What causes nephrotic syndrome in children?
faqs.answer: More than 85% of childhood nephrotic syndrome is caused by minimal change disease, a podocyte injury that responds to corticosteroids in 80-90% of cases. Less common causes include focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), membranous nephropathy, hereditary podocyte gene mutations, and infections.

faqs.question: What causes nephrotic syndrome in adults?
faqs.answer: In adults, common causes include membranous nephropathy, focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), minimal change disease, diabetic kidney disease, lupus nephritis, amyloidosis, and viral infections (HIV, hepatitis B and C). Diabetic kidney disease is the single most common cause globally.

faqs.question: How is nephrotic syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is based on a 24-hour urine collection or spot urine protein/creatinine ratio above 3.5, serum albumin under 3.0 g/dL, and clinical edema. Adults usually require a kidney biopsy to identify the underlying cause. Blood tests screen for diabetes, lupus, viral infections, and PLA2R antibodies.

faqs.question: Is nephrotic syndrome curable?
faqs.answer: Childhood minimal change disease has an excellent prognosis with 80-90% achieving complete remission with prednisolone, though relapses are common. Adult primary nephrotic syndromes such as FSGS and membranous nephropathy can achieve remission with immunosuppression in 50-65% of patients, with long-term kidney preservation strongly tied to response.

faqs.question: What is the first-line treatment for nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment depends on cause. Pediatric minimal change disease receives prednisolone 60 mg/m²/day. Adult primary membranous nephropathy now receives rituximab (MENTOR trial). FSGS receives high-dose prednisone. All patients receive ACE inhibitor or ARB, salt restriction, statin, and consideration of anticoagulation.

faqs.question: Why does nephrotic syndrome cause swelling?
faqs.answer: Heavy urinary protein loss lowers blood albumin, reducing the oncotic pressure that holds fluid in blood vessels. Fluid leaks into tissues, producing pitting edema in the ankles, eyes, and abdomen. Sodium retention from activated renin-angiotensin and sympathetic systems further worsens fluid overload.

faqs.question: Can nephrotic syndrome cause blood clots?
faqs.answer: Yes. Loss of antithrombin III and other coagulation regulators in urine, combined with increased clotting factor synthesis, makes nephrotic syndrome strongly prothrombotic. Renal-vein thrombosis is particularly common in membranous nephropathy (up to 30%). Prophylactic anticoagulation is used when serum albumin falls below 2.0-2.5 g/dL.

faqs.question: What is the difference between nephritic and nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Nephrotic syndrome features heavy proteinuria, low albumin, edema, hyperlipidemia, and a bland urinary sediment. Nephritic syndrome features hematuria, red-cell casts, hypertension, and reduced glomerular filtration from inflammation, with milder proteinuria. They can overlap in some glomerular diseases.

faqs.question: Is foamy urine always nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: No. Persistent foamy urine that does not clear with flushing is suspicious for significant proteinuria and warrants a urine dipstick or protein/creatinine ratio. Transient foam can occur with concentrated urine or fast urine stream. A urine PCR test confirms or excludes nephrotic-range protein loss.

faqs.question: How is membranous nephropathy treated?
faqs.answer: Modern first-line treatment is rituximab 1 g IV ×2 separated by 14 days, repeated at 6 months. KDIGO 2021 supports rituximab in moderate-to-high-risk disease. Alternatives include the modified Ponticelli regimen (cyclophosphamide-steroid) and calcineurin inhibitors. Anti-PLA2R titres are followed to assess response.

faqs.question: What is FSGS?
faqs.answer: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis is a glomerular disease characterized by scarring in some glomeruli and parts of glomeruli. It causes 20-25% of adult primary nephrotic syndrome. Primary FSGS is a podocytopathy; secondary FSGS arises from hyperfiltration, viral infection, drugs, or genetic podocyte mutations.

faqs.question: How long does treatment for nephrotic syndrome take?
faqs.answer: Pediatric minimal change disease responds within 2-4 weeks of starting steroids; the full course extends 4-6 months with taper. Adult FSGS requires 12-16 weeks of high-dose steroids. Membranous nephropathy responds over 12-24 months. Maintenance therapy and surveillance often continue for years.

faqs.question: Can nephrotic syndrome be cured without steroids?
faqs.answer: Steroids remain first-line in childhood minimal change disease because of high response rates. Alternative agents such as rituximab, calcineurin inhibitors, and mycophenolate are options in steroid-resistant, steroid-dependent, or frequently relapsing cases. Diabetic and viral-related nephrotic syndromes are treated by addressing the underlying cause rather than with steroids.

faqs.question: Does nephrotic syndrome lead to kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Risk depends on cause and treatment response. Pediatric minimal change disease rarely progresses to kidney failure. Adult FSGS progresses in 30-50% over 10 years. Untreated membranous nephropathy progresses in roughly one-third of cases. Achieving complete or partial remission with treatment substantially improves long-term kidney survival.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Moderate aerobic activity is safe and encouraged once major edema is controlled. Avoid contact sports if anticoagulated due to bleeding risk. Walking, cycling, and swimming are appropriate. Resistance training helps counteract corticosteroid-induced muscle loss in patients on prolonged immunosuppression.

faqs.question: What diet is best for nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Strict sodium restriction below 2 g per day reduces edema. Protein intake of 0.8-1.0 g/kg/day is appropriate for most patients; very high protein diets can worsen glomerular hyperfiltration. Limit added sugars and saturated fats given hyperlipidemia. Hydration with 1.5-2 L water daily unless restricted.

faqs.question: Should I get vaccinated if I have nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pneumococcal (PCV13 then PPSV23), influenza, hepatitis B, COVID-19, and varicella vaccinations are recommended, ideally before starting major immunosuppression. Live vaccines are generally avoided during active immunosuppression; planning vaccinations with the nephrology team is important.

faqs.question: Can nephrotic syndrome come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pediatric minimal change disease relapses in 60-70% of children within the first year; 30% become frequent relapsers. FSGS, membranous nephropathy, and lupus nephritis all carry relapse risk, particularly when immunosuppression is tapered. Anti-PLA2R titres can predict relapse in membranous nephropathy.

faqs.question: Is nephrotic syndrome hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most cases are not directly inherited. Congenital and infantile nephrotic syndromes have specific genetic causes (NPHS1, NPHS2, LAMB2, WT1). Adult FSGS shows increased risk with APOL1 high-risk variants in Black populations. Genetic testing is offered in early-onset, syndromic, or steroid-resistant disease.
